questions.question: Order the steps of the LeDoux two-pathway 'bear on a forest trail' fear response:
items: Cortex performs detailed analysis and refines/suppresses the response
Sensory input arrives at thalamus
Low road: thalamus → amygdala primes a fast defensive response
Intercalated cells gate signals; central amygdala drives hypothalamus and midbrain
correct_order: Sensory input arrives at thalamus
Low road: thalamus → amygdala primes a fast defensive response
Cortex performs detailed analysis and refines/suppresses the response
Intercalated cells gate signals; central amygdala drives hypothalamus and midbrain
questions.explanation: The thalamus sends sensory input simultaneously down the fast low road (priming the amygdala) and up the slower high road (cortical analysis). Intercalated cells then gate the central amygdala's output to hypothalamus (heart rate, breathing) and midbrain/striatum (action selection).

questions.question: Order the steps of fear acquisition and extinction over time:
items: Fear extinction: CS presented without US, new competing associations form
Initial CS-US pairing creates conditioned fear response
Without vmPFC brake, emotional perseveration persists
vmPFC applies inhibitory brake, suppressing amygdala output
correct_order: Initial CS-US pairing creates conditioned fear response
Fear extinction: CS presented without US, new competing associations form
vmPFC applies inhibitory brake, suppressing amygdala output
Without vmPFC brake, emotional perseveration persists
questions.explanation: Acquisition pairs CS with US to create the fear response. Extinction layers new competing associations on top (it does NOT erase the original). The vmPFC then applies the brake on the amygdala. If vmPFC is damaged, fear perseveres — the hallmark of anxiety, phobias, and PTSD.

questions.question: Order the HPA axis cascade:
items: Cortisol released from adrenal cortex
ACTH released from anterior pituitary
Stressful event triggers hypothalamus
CRF released from hypothalamus
correct_order: Stressful event triggers hypothalamus
CRF released from hypothalamus
ACTH released from anterior pituitary
Cortisol released from adrenal cortex
questions.explanation: HPA axis cascade: stress → hypothalamus releases CRF → anterior pituitary releases ACTH → adrenal cortex releases cortisol. Because hormones are slow, they primarily affect consolidation rather than encoding — explaining why memory of a stressful moment is sometimes patchy at the time but vivid later.

questions.question: Order the steps of the Iowa Gambling Task as healthy controls perform it:
items: After ~80 trials, controls verbally articulate which decks are bad
Anticipatory SCR develops before reaching for A/B
Participants begin sampling all four decks roughly equally
Behaviour shifts toward advantageous decks C/D
correct_order: Participants begin sampling all four decks roughly equally
Anticipatory SCR develops before reaching for A/B
Behaviour shifts toward advantageous decks C/D
After ~80 trials, controls verbally articulate which decks are bad
questions.explanation: Controls first explore all decks, then their bodies start producing anticipatory SCRs before the disadvantageous A/B picks (somatic marker), behaviour shifts to C/D, and finally explicit verbal knowledge appears. vmPFC patients never develop the SCR or the shift.

questions.question: Order the events of the propranolol experiment:
items: Compare memory: control vs propranolol vs amygdala-lesion
Subjects view audio-visual narrative with emotional middle (car crash) and neutral framing
Participants given propranolol (β-adrenergic blocker) or placebo
Result: propranolol mimics amygdala lesion — no emotional memory boost
correct_order: Participants given propranolol (β-adrenergic blocker) or placebo
Subjects view audio-visual narrative with emotional middle (car crash) and neutral framing
Compare memory: control vs propranolol vs amygdala-lesion
Result: propranolol mimics amygdala lesion — no emotional memory boost
questions.explanation: Propranolol or placebo is given before the narrative; participants then view the emotional/neutral material; later memory is tested across groups; the result confirms β-adrenergic action mediates the amygdala's enhancement of emotional memory consolidation.

questions.question: Order the steps of the Ochsner reappraisal experiment top-down model:
items: Amygdala and insula activity is dampened
Negative image presented under 'decrease' instructions
Dorsal frontoparietal network selects the reappraisal strategy
Dorsal ACC, vlPFC, dlPFC translate strategy into regulatory control signals
correct_order: Negative image presented under 'decrease' instructions
Dorsal frontoparietal network selects the reappraisal strategy
Dorsal ACC, vlPFC, dlPFC translate strategy into regulatory control signals
Amygdala and insula activity is dampened
questions.explanation: When asked to reappraise a negative image, the dorsal frontoparietal attentional network selects the strategy, dorsal ACC/vlPFC/dlPFC translate it into control signals, and those signals dampen amygdala and insula activity — reducing self-reported negative affect.

questions.question: Order the developmental trajectory of empathy:
items: Adult: fully developed, controlled empathy with self/other distinction
Infant: automatic emotional contagion (hearing one infant cry triggers others)
Child: egocentric sympathy ('I'd comfort them with what comforts me')
correct_order: Infant: automatic emotional contagion (hearing one infant cry triggers others)
Child: egocentric sympathy ('I'd comfort them with what comforts me')
Adult: fully developed, controlled empathy with self/other distinction
questions.explanation: Empathy develops automatic-to-controlled: infants show contagion, children offer egocentric comfort, and adults achieve regulated empathy that distinguishes self from other. Without the regulation step, automatic mirroring produces personal distress, not helpful empathy.

questions.question: Order the steps of the Sally-Anne false-belief task:
items: Sally returns and looks for her object
Sally places object in container A and leaves
Child is asked: 'Where will Sally look?'
Anne moves the object from A to B while Sally is away
correct_order: Sally places object in container A and leaves
Anne moves the object from A to B while Sally is away
Sally returns and looks for her object
Child is asked: 'Where will Sally look?'
questions.explanation: The Sally-Anne location-change task tests whether the child can attribute a false belief to Sally. Around age 4, children correctly answer 'A' (Sally believes the object is still where she left it) — the 'acid test' for explicit Theory of Mind.

questions.question: Order the amygdala feedback loop on visual processing:
items: Fusiform gyrus shows enhanced activation for fearful faces
Visual input ascends ventral stream to inferior temporal cortex
Amygdala receives late-stage visual input and detects emotional salience
Amygdala feeds back to early visual areas including V1
correct_order: Visual input ascends ventral stream to inferior temporal cortex
Amygdala receives late-stage visual input and detects emotional salience
Amygdala feeds back to early visual areas including V1
Fusiform gyrus shows enhanced activation for fearful faces
questions.explanation: Visual input climbs the ventral stream → amygdala detects emotional salience → amygdala feeds back to V1 and fusiform → fusiform shows enhanced response to fearful faces (e.g., fearful eye-whites). Patients with amygdala damage lack this fusiform enhancement, proving causation.

questions.question: Order the McGaugh memory modulation pathway from event to enhanced memory:
items: Stress hormones (cortisol, epinephrine, norepinephrine) released into bloodstream
Hormones act on β-adrenergic receptors and enhance MTL/PFC consolidation
Emotionally arousing event occurs
Amygdala activated; sends direct projections to MTL
correct_order: Emotionally arousing event occurs
Amygdala activated; sends direct projections to MTL
Stress hormones (cortisol, epinephrine, norepinephrine) released into bloodstream
Hormones act on β-adrenergic receptors and enhance MTL/PFC consolidation
questions.explanation: Memory modulation has two routes: a fast direct one (amygdala axonal projections to MTL/dlPFC/vlPFC) and a slow indirect hormonal one (cortisol crossing the BBB; epinephrine/norepinephrine on peripheral β-adrenergic receptors). Both converge to enhance consolidation of the emotional event.
